ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for Bhindi Masala with Tandoori Roti. FOODON_03311737 (okra dish) used as closest parent for the bhindi masala component. Tandoori roti component not separately coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Okra, prepared) used as closest parent for the bhindi masala component. No composite dish code exists for bhindi masala with tandoori roti in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for bhindi masala with tandoori roti as a composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving consists of approximately 200 g of bhindi masala curry alongside two tandoori roti (approximately 75 g each, totalling 150 g), giving a combined plate weight of around 350 g.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Bhindi (okra) preparation and Tandoori Roti (whole wheat flatbread)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Composite nutrient values derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition). Bhindi masala component based on ICMR-NIN cooked okra/vegetable curry data; tandoori roti component based on ICMR-NIN whole wheat chapati/roti data. Weighted average calculated for a combined dish at approximately 57% bhindi masala and 43% tandoori roti by weight, reflecting a standard restaurant plate. Values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for okra (NDB 11278) and whole wheat flatbread (NDB 18963) for plausibility.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — chapati (whole wheat flatbread) and mixed vegetable curry analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for bhindi masala with tandoori roti as a composite dish. GI estimated at 52 (low) based on: (1) tandoori roti / whole wheat chapati GI of approximately 52–62 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2021, Diabetes Care); (2) bhindi masala (okra-based vegetable curry) has a negligible independent GI contribution due to low carbohydrate content and high fibre from okra. Composite GI weighted toward the roti component which provides the majority of glycaemic carbohydrate. GL per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (52 × 71.75 g carbohydrate in 350 g serving) / 100 ≈ 37.3; however, accounting for the fibre-adjusted available carbohydrate of approximately 36.3 g net carbohydrate, GL = (52 × 36.3) / 100 ≈ 18.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from spice blends and cooking salt
hypertension.guidance: A standard serving of bhindi masala with tandoori roti provides approximately 1085 mg sodium, which represents nearly half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size to one roti.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycaemic load from whole wheat tandoori roti carbohydrate
type2_diabetes.guidance: The composite dish has a low GI of approximately 52 driven by whole wheat roti, but the glycaemic load per full serving is moderate at approximately 18.9; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit to one tandoori roti per serving and increase the proportion of bhindi masala, which contributes dietary fibre and has minimal glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high dietary fibre from okra, plant-based dish
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Bhindi masala with tandoori roti is a plant-based, low-saturated-fat dish with approximately 0.7 g saturated fat per 100 g and meaningful dietary fibre from okra (approximately 3.2 g per 100 g), making it a heart-favourable choice when prepared with minimal oil and moderate sodium.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bhindi Masala with Tandoori Roti is a classic North Indian vegetarian meal combination consisting of two components: bhindi masala, a dry or semi-dry spiced okra (lady's finger) stir-fry, and tandoori roti, a whole wheat flatbread baked in a clay tandoor oven. Bhindi masala is prepared by stir-frying fresh okra with onions, tomatoes, cumin seeds, coriander powder, turmeric, red chilli powder, and garam masala in oil until the okra is cooked through and the masala coats each piece. The dish has a slightly earthy, mildly spiced flavour with a tender yet slightly firm texture from the okra. Tandoori roti is made from whole wheat atta dough, shaped into rounds, and baked directly on the inner wall of a tandoor at high heat, producing a lightly charred, chewy flatbread with a subtle smoky flavour. Together, the combination provides a nutritionally balanced vegetarian meal with complex carbohydrates from the roti, dietary fibre and micronutrients from the okra, and moderate protein. Okra is a notable source of dietary fibre, folate, vitamin C, calcium, and iron. The dish is naturally vegan, halal, and dairy-free. Clinically, the composite dish has a low glycaemic index of approximately 52 and a moderate glycaemic load per serving, making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose when portion size is controlled. Sodium content is moderate to high depending on restaurant preparation. This dish is commonly served at Indian vegetarian restaurants in Singapore and across the Indian subcontinent.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) using a weighted composite of bhindi masala and tandoori roti components. No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for this exact combined dish. GI is an analogous estimate based on whole wheat chapati GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high because the values represent a calculated composite rather than a direct measurement of this specific restaurant dish.
notes: 1. This record represents the combined dish 'Bhindi Masala with Tandoori Roti' as served at Jewel of India, Boat Quay, Singapore (GrabFood listing GD002055). The original GrabFood food_name included a heart emoji and the phrase 'Free Tandoori Roti', indicating the tandoori roti is included complimentary with the bhindi masala order; the canonical food name has been normalised to 'Bhindi Masala with Tandoori Roti'. 2. The input food_group was listed as 'Grains and staples'; this has been corrected to 'Mixed' as the dish is a composite of a vegetable curry and a flatbread. 3. Nutrient values are a weighted composite estimate (57% bhindi masala, 43% tandoori roti by weight) derived from ICMR-NIN data; actual restaurant values may vary depending on oil quantity, salt, and portion size. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero for a vegan preparation. 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a vegan dish containing no animal products; confirmed zero. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as the dish uses standard vegetable oil without hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero for a traditional preparation. 7. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as vitamin D is not reported in ICMR-NIN for this food category and is not expected to be a meaningful contributor in a plant-based curry and flatbread dish. 8. selenium_mcg is -1 as selenium is not reported in ICMR-NIN for this food category. 9. GI estimated at 52 (low) based on whole wheat chapati analogue from University of Sydney GI Database; GL per serving approximately 18.9 based on 350 g serving with net available carbohydrate of approximately 36.3 g. 10. Aliases consistency verified: all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases list.

improvement_actions_taken: Normalised food_name from GrabFood menu string 'Bhindi Masala ❤️ Free Tandoori Roti' to canonical 'Bhindi Masala with Tandoori Roti'.
Corrected food_group from 'Grains and staples' to 'Mixed' to reflect composite vegetable curry and flatbread nature.
Added all mandatory multilingual local names: Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi.
Derived composite nutrient values from ICMR-NIN using weighted average of bhindi masala and tandoori roti components.
Estimated GI at 52 using whole wheat chapati analogue from University of Sydney GI Database with full documentation.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, selenium_mcg) and all 0.0 confirmed-zero values (vitamin_b12_mcg, cholesterol_mg, trans_fat_g) in notes.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list consistency; all entries match verbatim.
Selected closest_parent ontology codes with gap notes; no codes invented.
